attributes.stem:
A 70-year-old man is day 6 post elective right total hip arthroplasty. His operation was uneventful. His warfarin, taken for atrial fibrillation, was temporarily ceased prior to the operation and has since been re-commenced. He has been bridged on an unfractionated heparin infusion during this time while the INR is still sub-therapeutic. Today, he is complaining of intense right calf pain. He denies chest pain or dyspnoea.

On examination, he is alert but in obvious pain. His right calf appears swollen and is warm and tender on palpation.

His blood tests show:

  • Haemoglobin 130 g/L [115-155]

  • Platelets 30 x 10^9/L [150-450]

  • White cell count 10.2 x 10^9/L [4.0-10.0]

  • INR 1.8 [0.9-1.2]

  • aPTT 90 seconds [24-38]
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate initial management approach?
options: Transfuse platelets to target >50, continue heparin and warfarin
Change to prophylactic heparin, continue warfarin
Stop heparin and warfarin, start fondaparinux
Replace heparin and warfarin with enoxaparin
attributes.explanation:
This patient's symptoms suggest a deep vein thrombosis, in the setting of heparin administration and new-onset thrombocytopenia, which is concerning for heparin-induced thrombocytopenia. The most immediate management for this is the cessation of heparin and the administration of an alternate anticoagulant.

Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) or heparin induced thrombotic thrombocytopenia Syndrome (HITTS) is a life-threatening, autoimmune complication of exposure to heparin. HITTS results from the production of autoantibodies directed against platelet factor 4 in complex with heparin. This leads to the activation of platelets and thrombocytopenia, as well as catastrophic arterial and venous thrombosis.

The clinical presentation of HITTS is variable and the condition may occur with any heparin dose, schedule or route of administration, however, there is a greater incidence of HITTS in patients receiving unfractionated heparin, as opposed to low molecular weight heparin. HITTS typically occurs 5-10 days after the initiation of heparin, while clinical manifestations may include haemorrhage, thrombosis, as well as sequelae of thrombosis such as skin necrosis, limb gangrene or organ ischaemia.

Diagnosis of HITTS is based on clinical and laboratory findings and should be suspected in a heparinised patient with new-onset thrombocytopenia or the clinical symptoms of HITTS. The 4Ts score may be used to calculate the pretest probability of HITTS. It includes:

  • Thrombocytopenia

  • Timing after heparin exposure [5-10 days]

  • Thrombosis or other clinical features

  • No other cause of thrombocytopenia

In HITTS, values for aPTT and INR may also be increased, and therefore it is advisable to obtain baseline values to avoid confounding and treatment failure.

Immunologic testing including detection of platelet-factor-4 antibodies and the serotonin release assay may be used to support the diagnosis.

Treatment of HITTS should involve the cessation of all formulations of heparin and careful monitoring of clinical features and platelet count. Whilst low-molecular weight formulations of heparin, such as Enoxaparin, have a lower risk of causing HITTS, they are still contraindicated in patients who have active or past HITTS due to their role in triggering or perpetuating HITTS pathophysiology. Alternative anticoagulation should be initiated immediately, options include fondaparinux or argatroban in the acute setting, followed by the transition to a direct-acting oral anticoagulant (DOAC) or warfarin. Warfarin should not be used until the platelet count has normalised as it may exacerbate the imbalance between anticoagulant and procoagulant proteins seen in HITTS. In the absence of heparin exposure, the platelet count will progressively recover.

It is worthwhile to denote that fondaparinux is a non-heparin anticoagulant and is considered a high-risk medication. It should be used with high caution and its main indication is for HITTS.
learningPoints: Treatment of Heparin Induced Thrombotic Thrombocytopaenia Syndrome (HITTS) should involve the cessation of all formulations of heparin and careful monitoring of clinical features and platelet count.
Fondaparinux is a non-heparin anticoagulant and its main indication is for HITTS, though it should be noted that this is a high-risk medication.
references: Ahmed I, Majeed A, Powell R. Heparin induced thrombocytopenia: diagnosis and management update. Postgrad Med J. 2007;83(983):575-82.

Cuker A, Arepally GM, Chong BH, Cines DB, Greinacher A, Gruel Y, et al. American Society of Hematology 2018 guidelines for management of venous thromboembolism: heparin-induced thrombocytopenia. Blood Adv. 2018;2(22):3360-92.
Lee GM, Arepally GM. Diagnosis and management of heparin-induced thrombocytopenia. Hematol Oncol Clin North Am. 2013;27(3):541-63.
Nagler M, Bachmann LM, ten Cate H, ten Cate-Hoek A. Diagnostic value of immunoassays for heparin-induced thrombocytopenia: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Blood. 2016;127(5):546-57.

Salter BS, Weiner MM, Trinh MA, Heller J, Evans AS, Adams DH, et al. Heparin-Induced Thrombocytopenia: A Comprehensive Clinical Review. Journal of the American College of Cardiology. 2016;67(21):2519-32.

NSW Health. Medicine Guideline for the Safe Use of FONDAPARINUX. NSW Government.  2023. Available from: https://www.seslhd.health.nsw.gov.au/sites/default/files/documents/Medicine%20Guideline%20-%20Fondaparinux%20for%20Heparin%20induced%20Thrombocytopenia%20%28HIT%29.pdf
